speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four of Barbara Traub's interview. So the order came down.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first, I believe, the first four streets or five streets-- I don't know exactly, but it was clear that this was just a section of the ghetto-- that we have to pack. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ghetto is being liquidated. Now what was interesting was the panic-- that why was the ghetto supposed to be liquidated? Nobody was saying. And the policemen, Jewish policemen, wouldn't say. Maybe they didn't know. And it-- maybe I hated them at the time. But, as I look back, I'm glad that none of my brothers were Jewish policemen because they would have had to do exactly what those Jewish policeman did, get us out. But what happened was that somehow, we thought, or people thought, that if we would somehow run to the Fifth Street or the Sixth Street, which was not included in this order, we would be saved. Well, some people did, only tragically, not to be saved. But we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just mentioning this, that there was this element of trying to, to save yourself without knowing what, why. But maybe there it's going to be better. It was chaos. It was horror. It was fear-- the unknown, why, why, why? All right, now-- How much and what information was flowing into the ghetto in terms of rumors or news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, because the only thing-- and it was almost like, like a drug, like morphine or something. The cannons across the mountains-- the Russians are going to liberate us. And some religious Jews didn't feel too good about the Russians being communists. But all right, I mean, all right, even if they are the communists, but they will free us, liberate us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we are Jews. And we will worship our God and, and, and, and, and, and be Jews and live by our religion. So? So it's the Russian communists who will liberate us. So there was so much hope pinned on that that the rumors, you know, didn't reach us, didn't reach us. And furthermore-- of course, I say this with hindsight-- when reality becomes unbearable, you deny it. You just deny it. And you think of the cannons across the mountains. And they were there. They were there, no question.

speaker: interviewee
text: The 12 hours that they gave us advance notice gave most Jews, as it did my mother, respite in the sense to prepare. We didn't know where, what, why. But this is what was happening in that little room where we were. My mother, my, sister-in-law, and I-- and the cook this time-- were preparing, sewing some jewelry, diamond rings, necklaces-- God knows-- you know, that sort of precious things-- and gold coins, banknotes into my mother's, my sister's, and my lining. My lining, no. I got the jewelry in the shoulder pads of a raincoat.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember my mother had a very beautiful pair of big diamond earrings. You know where that went? Into a jar of marmalade that I remember the cook carrying-- and I'll tell you later where she carried it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came the next morning a knock on the door. And this is in the ghetto. And this time, all we could take was a valise. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they take us? Well, they took us to the big synagogue, because there was the-- the big orthodox synagogue, which was the big-- it was a big building. And it accommodated over a thousand or 2,000 people. There, they-- there were Hungarian policemen, Hungarian policewomen who carried out about a body search. It was a horrible night at the synagogue, children crying, people on the floor, not knowing why. Why are they liquidating the ghetto? Nobody says anything. We don't know. We don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came next morning-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the gendarmes this time, Hungarian gendarmes appear on the scene. And they whip us into a row. The row starts going towards the train station. By the time the first row arrives at the train station, there is still this winding row which is still at the synagogue. There were 4,000 people, as we learned later. What they did, they divided the Sziget Jewry in four transports. We were the first. Now we come to the train station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is another painful memory because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we come to the train station. And all I can think of is the trains I used to watch from my mother house across the field miles and miles. But they were plain, level fields. And twice a day, there was a train which, in the distance, went by. And I heard its, its whistle. And as a child, I used to run out. And it was a delightful thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I kept saying that this-- I mean, it's trains. I used to love to listen to the whistle. What is going-- what? Why? Because now we see about 40-- I don't know whether it was 40 or 20, I really don't know-- but I see, we see a number of cattle trains, open cattle trains. Why? What are we doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the screaming and the yelling and the truncheons and the hitting and the gendarmes-- and now, for the first time, we are surrounded by SS officers. And the screaming-- and it is spring. And I can literally have the scent of the lilac in my nostrils. It's early in the morning. The sun shines. It's beautiful. And children cry. And, and, and and, and, old, sick people are kicked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And SS-- we are surrounded by SS. And we don't know what's going on. Where, where, where? Where are you taking us? Finally, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a Hungarian gendarme says, all right, I'll tell you where we we are-- where, where you are going to be taken. You are going to be taken to a nice transit camp in western Hungary called 'Kenyermezo.' Kenyermezo, in Hungarian, means 'field of bread.' Lovely name, right? Why? Well, you see, the gendarme says, the Russians are very close. And you know they are godless people. And they may harm you Jews. So we've decided that we'll keep you in this transit camp in Kenyermezo until war's end. And then you'll return to your homes. A sigh of relief, right? Lovely name, Kenyermezo.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, 'schnell!.' this was the first time I heard the word 'schnell,' 'schnell,' pushing into the wagons, into the cattle car. This time, Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were barking. They were kicking. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were terrifying to look at. It was the first time I saw the-- the skull and crossbones and the Nazi insignia on their collar. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They looked to me like men-- like creatures made of stone. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We were pushed into the wagon, into the cattle car, my mother and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister-in-law there?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my sister-in-law and the children. And it was two nights-- two days and three nights of horror because there was no food. Whatever food there was was eventually consumed. But the thirst became awful, became dreadful. I remember particularly that thirst was the worst thing. I remember as the train would stop, and while the train was still in Hungary, peasant women, Hungarian peasant women, would come out with pails of water and try to sell it. And I remember I climbed-- because there was a little barred window-- climbed there. And I was showing a ring. And she was ready to reach to give me the water. And then an SS kicked the bucket, the pail. And that was it. But it was still-- and it was terrible. But there was still a certain amount of-- of civilized behavior because there were two buckets there for sanitary purposes. So whenever somebody would have to use it, there was still some reluctance, some attempt at privacy by two people holding a blanket. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it was foul. It stunk. Children crying-- my little niece, Eva, who was about four weeks old, the mother didn't have any more milk. But still, we'll get to Kenyermezo, and then this misery is going to be over. Except that one night, a neighbor of ours, whom I knew by the name of Mr. Weinfeld, who originally came from Poland, and who had the good sense at the time the Hungarians marched in, to make sure he had Hungarian citizenship-- although what difference it made-- by now, I really realize that it made none. He said one night in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: They looked at him. What are you talking about? We are going to Kenyermezo. He said, [YIDDISH]. I can smell the Tatra air. The Tatra is where an extension of the Carpathian mountains, which extended into Poland. He says, if we would have been going to Kenyermezo, that's in Western Hungary, plain fields, no mountains. This is a different air. What was the first part of his quote?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. I don't remember how you say 'Hungary' in Yiddish. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. That's what's he said. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are in Poland. [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Translate?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? [YIDDISH], Jews, we are in Poland. Woe unto us Jews. Because Poland, as the whole world knew, was occupied by Germany. Because Poland, as the whole world knew, was the place where they quote, 'resettled,' unquote, Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you know at that point--

speaker: interviewee
text: About Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: --about the truth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nothing, nothing, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The war was almost over. And God, America, and England was going to save us, eventually God and the Russian army. And then, he was right, clearly, because then the train stopped. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the doors were yanked open. It was night. And strange creatures to me then, strange male creatures in striped garbs, pajama-like things with funny, striped hats on their head, began yelling, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We didn't know who they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: We kept asking, where, where? One of them said, in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. Don't ask. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They pushed us out.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were dead people in the cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember how many. But I do remember, as I look back, because they were kicking, clearly, corpses-- several. What I personally remember is that horrible thirst. And I wanted to drink the air. And I opened my mouth. And I took a deep breath. And I almost vomited or choked because the air was foul. It stunk. It had a strange odor. It was awful. I didn't know what it was. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think it was? What did it smell like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think, I didn't know. I just didn't know. I just knew I'm, I'm going to suffocate. And it was dark. And now and then, the sky suddenly was lit by flames. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know what it was. And I didn't care to know. I didn't care to think. I was thirsty. I was frightened. They were pushing us, men and women. And I wanted to hold onto my mother and to my sister-in-law and to the nieces. And I didn't know what the flames were. And I didn't know why, why the air was so putrid. I didn't know. And they were pushing us and pushing us till eventually they-- these were the striped-garbed prisoners, men. Eventually they separated the men from the women. And they somehow whipped us into a column, a column. And they were getting us somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there SS around as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Were there SS? As a matter of fact, yes. You see, it was dark. But what I do remember, what comes back to me right now, are the long canes, the long sticks with-- the curved-neck sticks. And the SS had that. And yes. And I remember the barks-- the dogs. And that's when-- that's right-- the striped men just got us out the train. The SS separated the men and the women. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were now whipped into this column-- five. It was-- I was the first one, my mother, my sister-in-law with the two children, and Mr. and Mrs. Weinfeld, another couple. And they were whipping us and driving us toward someplace. It was dark. And as I said, it was just the smell and the flames. And we were now, by this time, we were surrounded by the SS. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember word got around that-- at the head of the column, one of the SS said that whoever is going to be caught with hidden contraband, if you will, money or diamonds or any-- because everything had to be left-- oh, I forgot to tell you. The order was-- and this is what the Jewish-- well, as I later found out that these were [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], prisoners, Jews. They said, leave everything in the cattle train. Leave everything in the cattle train. Don't take anything along because it's dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all we left were the valises. Now word comes down that the SS says, any jewelry, anything of the sort, whoever is going to be caught with that is going to be shot. So I take out my shoulder pads. My mother rips her lining. So does my sister-in-law. And a fortune is thrown into the ditches. And we march. I think I'm-- Was the cook with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. The cook was behind us. But she held on-- because that's the last picture I have of that poor cook walking. I'll get to that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know now that that march, it was a two-, three-mile-long march. And now we come into a big square, illuminated square, powerful reflectors all around, where on some sort of a podium, there stands this SS man. He looked like a giant, handsome, with his coat like a cape thrown around his shoulders. And the row of women passes before him. And he has what looks like a riding crop in his hand. And he looks. And he does this to the right, that to the left. And some women go to the right. Some women go to the left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, as the column advances, we come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, as I said, I was first, my mother, my sister-in-law, and that other couple. And he looks at us. And again, I remember as we were walking, my mother-- who even then, wore a black kerchief because she was mourning, mourning, mourning after my sister-- turns to me and says to me, Babuka, if you have a kerchief, put it on your head. I don't want those bad people to see how pretty you are. So I had a scarf. And I put it on my head. And so when we approach this man, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who stands there as the incarnation of evil, big, powerful, with the crop, he looks at my mother. He looks at me. And I saw then that his eyes, they were darting like a metronome. It went like this, like this-- he was looking. He couldn't make up his mind, clearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, [GERMAN], meaning 'mother and daughter?' And we were law-abiding and truthful people. And we said, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I had this hand over my mother's shoulder. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And he tapped that shoulder over which my hand was. And he didn't tap his left, my hand. But he really know, I know now, that the hand didn't mean anything. But he was telling my mother, and he said, [GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My hand dropped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you know, for weeks, my hand, my right hand, what was my-- the inside of my right hand was ice. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He said, [GERMAN]. And my mother went to the left. And he said to me, [GERMAN]. And I went to the right. I looked around. My sister-in-law wasn't there, nobody. And then I saw my cook, our cook, with the pot, with the jar of marmalade in her hand. She was holding onto that. I saw her over there with my mother. But I did not see my mother. And I just remember, I mean, my mother suddenly disappeared, the children and my sister-in-law, little Anika, whom I loved so much, that little girl. And, and there was nobody that I knew, you know, after I was sent to the right. And there was an SS. And I don't know what made me do it. I really don't know, because if I would have thought about it, I wouldn't have dared do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I ran to him. And I began shaking him. I said, my Mutter, my Mutter, my mother, my mother. When am I going to see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, he said, in a very polite tone, ah, Fraulein, he said, [GERMAN]. Don't worry. You will see your mama [GERMAN] perhaps tomorrow. Surely the day after tomorrow, you will see your mama. Don't you worry. They are old. And we'll put them on trucks and take them to a camp where they will be comfortable. You know, at the time, it did not impact on me that this was the second biggest lie that ruined my life in a sense. Because the first big lie was the Kenyermezo lie, that we are taken somewhere else. But I tell you, those two lies have made me, to-- till this day, react so enormously, so powerfully, against lies, I cannot handle lies. I cannot handle lies. They do something to me chemically. Something happens in the chemistry of my body when I realize that somebody lied to me. But the SS told me, Fraulein, in a very polite tone. And so, I thought I'll see my mother the next day or the day after. Thank god, she wouldn't have to walk as we eventually had to walk for about another three or four miles. Because, as I know now, this was all in Auschwitz. And they were taking us to Birkenau. By the time we arrived to Birkenau, to this other camp-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, I am going to backtrack now because I suddenly remembered the 'Arbeit macht frei' sign. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Yeah, the 'Arbeit macht frei' sign-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] anyway, there was that sign, as you know, the motto of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us from the beginning about the sign.

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us from the beginning about the sign.

speaker: interviewee
text: The sign-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when they took us from the train to-- well, it was beginning to dawn, no, no, not quite dawn. And it's very strange. Well, they were the [GERMAN], the night workers. They were-- From the train, they took us through a gate. And I told you that the flames were shooting and really, illuminating much of the place. And I looked up. And there was a big, wrought-iron-- a big sign which said, 'Arbeit macht frei,' 'work gives you freedom.' Well. The mockery of it, it didn't hit me then. Well, that was our reception into Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think when you saw that sign?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what it meant. Arbeit macht frei? What-- well, we are supposed to work, but-- but this couldn't be Kenyermezo because the sign is in German. I mean, you know, people do nothing rationally when the panic, the fear, when the heartbeat is, is, is, is mad, when, when, when blood runs cold, you don't know what it is. It's another planet, another world.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've never seen these people. You've never-- you were never exposed to such smells, to such yells, to such cries. Where am I? It's a nightmare. No, it's not a nightmare. I'm awake. I don't know what the signs means, 'Arbeit macht frei.' I was supposed to be frei, to be a free person. I mean, what, what, what is it? I did not know. I don't think anybody knew at that time. Can we stop for a second?
